section_heading: De Novo Protein Binder Design for Advanced Glycation End Products: A Computational Approach to Targeting AgeRelated Glycative Damage (Stage 1 Registered Report)

claim: The top 100 - 200 computational designs will be validated experimentally through yeast surface display screening against CML-modified bovine serum albumin, and top candidates will undergo binding affinity characterization by biolayer interferometry.
This pipeline will yield at least one binder with measurable binding to CML-modified protein and sub-micromolar affinity.
No approved therapies specifically target AGEs.
Computational designs, experimental protocols, and raw data will be made openly available.

Advanced glycation end products (AGEs) accumulate with age and contribute to cardiovascular disease, neurodegeneration, and diabetic complications through receptor for advanced glycation end products (RAGE) activation and direct protein cross-linking.
Carboxymethyllysine (CML) is the most abundant and well-characterized AGE species.
Deep learning-based protein design will be applied to create de novo protein binders that sequester carboxymethyllysine (CML).
This work is the first application of RFdiffusion All-Atom to AGE targeting.
This work will establish whether computationally designed protein binders can serve as a new class of anti-glycation therapeutics.
AGEs form through non-enzymatic glycation when reducing sugars react with amino groups on proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids.
section_heading: Research Question: Background, Importance, and Relevance

claim: The Maillard reaction produces early glycation intermediates that undergo oxidation and rearrangement to form stable AGE adducts.
section_heading: Research Question: Background, Importance, and Relevance

claim: The most abundant and best-characterized AGEs include carboxymethyllysine (CML), carboxyethyllysine (CEL), and pentosidine.
section_heading: Research Question: Background, Importance, and Relevance

claim: These modifications accumulate in nearly all mammalian tissues with age and are accelerated by hyperglycemia, oxidative stress, and metabolic dysfunction.
section_heading: Research Question: Background, Importance, and Relevance

claim: AGE cross-links increase tissue stiffness and reduce turnover in long-lived extracellular matrix proteins like collagen.
section_heading: Research Question: Background, Importance, and Relevance

claim: AGEs cause pathology through two primary mechanisms: direct modification of proteins causing cross-linking that impairs function, and binding to the receptor for advanced glycation end products.
section_heading: Research Question: Background, Importance, and Relevance

claim: The RAGE-AGE axis has been implicated in diabetic complications, atherosclerosis, Alzheimer's disease, and accelerated aging.
section_heading: Research Question: Background, Importance, and Relevance

claim: Current therapeutic approaches for AGE-related indications fall into three categories: AGE formation inhibitors, AGE cross-link breakers, and RAGE antagonists.
section_heading: Research Question: Background, Importance, and Relevance

claim: None of the current therapeutic approaches have achieved regulatory approval for AGE-related indications.
section_heading: Research Question: Background, Importance, and Relevance

claim: Aminoguanidine reached Phase III trials but was discontinued due to adverse effects.
section_heading: Research Question: Background, Importance, and Relevance

claim: Natural polyphenols and dietary interventions lack specificity and potency in addressing AGE-related indications.
section_heading: Research Question: Background, Importance, and Relevance

claim: Activating RAGE signaling triggers chronic inflammation, oxidative stress, and cellular dysfunction.
section_heading: Research Question: Background, Importance, and Relevance

claim: This project proposes a novel strategy: designing de novo protein binders that recognize and sequester specific AGE adducts.
section_heading: Research Question: Background, Importance, and Relevance

claim: Unlike small molecules, designed proteins can achieve high affinity and specificity through extensive complementary surfaces.
section_heading: Research Question: Background, Importance, and Relevance

claim: This approach has been validated for other small molecule targets including digoxigenin and heme.
section_heading: Research Question: Background, Importance, and Relevance

claim: No group has yet applied this technology to AGE targeting.
section_heading: Research Question: Background, Importance, and Relevance

claim: A designed AGE binder could function therapeutically by intercepting circulating AGEs before they engage RAGE or cross-link tissue proteins.
section_heading: Research Question: Background, Importance, and Relevance

claim: Such binders would serve as research tools for detecting and quantifying specific AGE species, and as scaffolds for downstream enzyme design to actively degrade AGEs.
section_heading: Research Question: Background, Importance, and Relevance

claim: The study will be completed over 6 months following Stage 1 peer review approval.
Months 1 -2 will involve computational design and in silico screening including backbone generation, sequence design, and AlphaFold2/Rosetta filtering.
Months 3 -4 will focus on gene synthesis and yeast surface display library construction and expression.
Months 5 -6 will include binding validation through FACS screening, protein expression, purification, and affinity characterization by BLI.
A Stage 2 submission is anticipated approximately 8 months after Stage 1 acceptance to allow data analysis and manuscript preparation.
The Methods section contains sufficient detail to enable full replication of all computational and experimental procedures.
Carboxymethyllysine (CML) is the most abundant advanced glycation end product (AGE) in human tissues.
Carboxymethyllysine (CML) is chemically stable.
Carboxymethyllysine (CML) has commercially available standards.
The molecular structure of Carboxymethyllysine (CML) is well characterized.
The molecular structure of CML bound to a lysine backbone will be used as the design input for the study.
Coordinates for molecular structures can be generated from SMILES notation using RDKit (version 2023.09).
Molecular coordinates can be energy-minimized using the MMFF94 force field prior to diffusion design.
RFdiffusion All-Atom will generate 10,000 protein scaffolds of 70-100 residues conditioned on the CML structure, producing diverse binding pocket geometries.
ProteinMPNN will assign amino acid sequences to each backbone, optimizing for the predicted binding interface. Five independent sequences will be generated per backbone.
AlphaFold2 (v2.3) will be used to score designs for structural confidence (pLDDT) and Rosetta (REF2015 score function) for binding energy estimation.
The top 100-200 designs ranked by a combined metric of pLDDT > 85 and Rosetta binding energy will advance to experimental testing.
Designed binders will be displayed on yeast cell surfaces as Aga2p fusions.
A pooled library of top designs will be constructed through oligonucleotide synthesis and Gibson assembly.
The library will be sorted against biotinylated AGE-BSA using fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS).
Binding cells will be enriched over 2-3 rounds of selection.
Individual clones from the enriched population will be sequenced by Sanger sequencing and characterized.

Binding kinetics (kon, koff) and equilibrium affinity (KD) will be measured using biolayer interferometry (BLI) on an Octet RED96 instrument (Sartorius) with AGE-BSA immobilized on streptavidin biosensors.
Top hits from yeast display will be expressed in E. coli (BL21(DE3) strain) and purified by nickel affinity chromatography.
Specificity will be assessed by parallel testing against unmodified BSA at equivalent concentrations.
Blinding is not applicable to backbone generation or sequence design due to their computational nature.
FACS sorting will be conducted by a laboratory member who is not the principal investigator, and BLI data will be collected and processed prior to unblinding design identities.
All computational designs that meet pre-specified in silico filtering thresholds will proceed to experimental testing without exclusion criteria.
The experimental hit rate for top-filtered designs is estimated at 5 -15%.
Testing 100 -200 designs is expected to yield 5 -30 binders with detectable binding signal.
The estimate of 5-15% hit rate is conservative because AGE-BSA presents multiple CML epitopes per molecule, increasing effective target valency and concentration compared to single-epitope targets.
A hit rate of ≥ 1% (at least 1 binder out of 100 tested) would be considered evidence that the computational pipeline can identify functional binders for first-in-class platform development.
BLI measurements will use standard curves with at least 5 analyte concentrations per binder, fit to a 1:1 Langmuir binding model, to estimate binding constants.
The study is designed as a proof-of-concept screen rather than a hypothesis test with a predetermined power target.
No covariates or regressors are anticipated for primary analyses.

Data will be collected from four sources: RFdiffusion All-Atom output files (PDB format) for backbone designs, AlphaFold2 pLDDT scores and predicted aligned error (PAE) matrices, Rosetta binding energy scores, and BLI sensorgrams from the Octet RED96.
section_heading: Data Collection, Processing, and Planned Analysis Data Sources and Instruments

claim: FACS data will be collected on a BD FACSAria instrument and analyzed using FlowJo (v10).
section_heading: Data Collection, Processing, and Planned Analysis Data Sources and Instruments

claim: All raw data files will be retained in their original formats.
section_heading: Data Collection, Processing, and Planned Analysis Data Sources and Instruments

claim: Computational designs are filtered by requiring pLDDT > 85 across the full design prior to experimental testing.
Computational designs are filtered by requiring a predicted TM-score to the design model > 0.8 prior to experimental testing.
Computational designs are filtered by requiring no steric clashes with CML ligand as assessed by Rosetta fa_rep term < 5.0 prior to experimental testing.
Designs that fail any of the pre-processing criteria are excluded from experimental testing.
BLI sensorgrams will be double-referenced (reference channel and buffer-only reference) prior to kinetic fitting.
The Octet Analysis Studio software (Sartorius, v12) will be used for kinetic fitting of BLI sensorgrams.
Designs with a signal-to-background ratio greater than 3 relative to noligand controls are classified as hits.
The hit rate and its 95% Wilson confidence interval will be reported as the primary outcome.
The Spearman rank correlation assesses the correlation between in silico metrics (AlphaFold2 pLDDT, Rosetta binding energy) and experimental binding.
Structural diversity of successful binders is assessed by pairwise backbone RMSD clustering.
The affinity distribution of BLI-validated hits is reported as KD values with 95% confidence intervals from curve fitting.

If the experimental sample falls below 100 designs due to in silico filtering, all passing designs will be tested, and the hit rate will be reported with confidence intervals adjusted for the smaller sample size.
Gene synthesis failures (estimated at less than 5% of sequences) will be replaced with the next-ranked designs from the computational pipeline.
No imputation will be applied to missing BLI measurements; any binder for which a reliable fit cannot be obtained will be reported as 'fit failed' with the raw data provided.
Preliminary computational experiments confirm feasibility.
Test runs of RFdiffusion All-Atom with CML as the target ligand successfully generated diverse protein scaffolds with plausible binding pockets.
AlphaFold2 predicts high confidence (pLDDT > 85) for 15 -20% of designs after ProteinMPNN sequence assignment.
The absence of prior art applying RFdiffusion All-Atom to AGE targets justifies the absence of wet laboratory pilot data in this exploratory project.
Affinity values (KD) will be reported as mean ± standard deviation from triplicate measurements.

Secondary analyses (i) -(iii) are exploratory; no correction for multiple comparisons will be applied, and all p-values will be reported without adjustment with a note that these analyses are hypothesis-generating.
Hit rates from yeast display will be reported as proportions with 95% Wilson score confidence intervals.
Spearman rank correlation will be used for secondary analysis to avoid assumptions of normality given the expected right-skewed distribution of binding scores.
BLI kinetic data will be fit to a 1:1 Langmuir model using nonlinear least squares regression in Octet Analysis Studio.
Designs for which BLI fitting fails (R² < 0.95) will be excluded from affinity analyses but reported descriptively.
Outliers in BLI data will be defined as measurements deviating more than 3 standard deviations from the replicate mean and will be excluded after documentation.

If no designs show a signal-to-background ratio > 3 in the initial yeast display screen, then the following pre-specified contingent analyses will be executed: (a) testing binding to free CMLlysine conjugate to determine whether the absence of binding reflects a scaffold problem or a target accessibility problem; and (b) assessing whether designs adopt the computationally predicted fold using circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy to determine whether folding failures explain the absence of binding.
The contingent analyses will be clearly labeled as such in the Stage 2 report.

If H2 and H3 are supported (binding detected with sub-micromolar affinity), this will establish proof-of-concept for a new class of AGE-targeting protein therapeutics and produce binder sequences that can be optimized through directed evolution.

This would represent the first demonstration that computationally designed proteins can specifically engage a posttranslational glycation modification, advancing both the therapeutic and research tool landscape for aging biology.

If binding is not achieved (H2 not supported), the negative results will inform the field about the challenges of designing binders for post-translationally modified amino acids and guide future computational approaches.
Results from the contingent analyses will distinguish between scaffold folding failures and target accessibility as limiting factors, providing actionable direction for subsequent design iterations.
These findings will contribute to the literature on de novo protein design benchmarks and to the aging intervention field.
Successful development of anti-AGE biologics could eventually inform therapeutic strategies for diabetes complications and age-related diseases.

All computational designs, screening data, binding characterization results, and raw data files will be deposited on Zenodo under a Creative Commons Attribution (CC-BY 4.0) license within 3 months of project completion, regardless of outcome.
A preregistration will be maintained on ResearchHub, and results will be shared as a preprint on bioRxiv prior to journal submission.
The Stage 2 registered report will be submitted to ResearchHub.
Data collection has not started at the time of the Stage 1 submission.
Preliminary computational feasibility tests of RFdiffusion All-Atom with CML have been completed.
The full design generation campaign and experimental work will commence after Stage 1 peer review approval.
The author will conduct this work as part of ongoing postdoctoral research.
Computational infrastructure beyond cloud GPU is available through existing laboratory resources at the Baker Lab.
Cloud GPU computing costs approximate 1,000 USD.
Cloud GPU computing costs will be funded from discretionary postdoctoral research funds.
This work is supported by a ResearchHub Foundation grant.
No external grants are involved in supporting this specific piece of research.
No scientific claims identified in the text.
The project does not involve human subjects, vertebrate animals, or biohazardous materials.
The project uses commercially available reagents and standard molecular biology techniques with E. coli and Saccharomyces cerevisiae.
Institutional biosafety approval for routine E. coli and yeast work is already in place at the University of Washington.
No additional ethics committee approvals are required for this phase of the project.
This study is not a clinical trial and does not require trial registration.
A preregistration of this protocol will be posted on ResearchHub prior to commencement of data collection.
